data.title: Has SEB (ENXTPA:SK) Fallen Too Far Given Its DCF And P/E Valuations?
data.description:
• Wondering whether SEB at €47.26 is a bargain or a value trap right now? This article breaks down what the current price really implies.
  • The stock has risen 6.3% over the last 7 days and 0.5% over 30 days, although returns sit at a 4.2% decline year to date and a 35.0% decline over the last year.
  • Recent coverage has focused on SEB's share price performance and how investors are reassessing the company after multi year declines of 50.4% over 3 years and 62.7% over 5 years. This context matters because it frames the question of whether sentiment has moved further than the underlying business fundamentals.
  • SEB currently has a valuation score of 6/6. This sets the stage to compare different valuation methods next and then look at an even deeper way of thinking about value at the end of the article.

Find out why SEB's -35.0% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: SEB Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today in € terms.

For SEB, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of €0.7m, so the story here is built around future recovery and growth. Analysts provide detailed forecasts out to 2028, with free cash flow for that year projected at €541.1m. Simply Wall St then extrapolates cash flows further, with estimates reaching about €1,147.5m in 2035.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back, the resulting intrinsic value comes out at about €147.20 per share. Against the current share price of €47.26, this implies a 67.9% discount, which indicates that the shares are trading well below the DCF estimate of value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests SEB is undervalued by 67.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 240 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for SEB.

Approach 2: SEB Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is often a useful yardstick because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay for €1 of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E really depends on two big levers: how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are perceived to be. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to support a lower P/E.

SEB currently trades on a P/E of 10.59x. That compares with about 15.73x for the Consumer Durables industry and a peer group average of 18.34x. Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” of 16.76x for SEB. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E could be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, the company’s industry, market cap and specific risks. Because it blends these elements, it can offer a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry.

Set against this 16.76x Fair Ratio, SEB’s current 10.59x P/E sits meaningfully lower.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 96 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your SEB Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, and on Simply Wall St this comes through Narratives. You tell the story you believe about SEB, link it to explicit assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins, and the platform converts that into a fair value that you can compare with the current €47.26 share price. You can track this on the Community page and see it update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive. One investor might build a cautious SEB Narrative around the €60.0 fair value with lower P/E and higher perceived risks, while another leans toward the €102.0 upper analyst target with stronger growth and margin assumptions, and both can quickly see whether their own fair value suggests SEB is above or below a price where they would consider buying or selling.

data.title: AMD Takes Lead In UALink Standard As AI Cluster Role Grows
data.description:
• UALink Consortium has ratified its next generation open accelerator interconnect standards, with Advanced Micro Devices (NasdaqGS:AMD) in a leadership role.
  • The updated UALink Specification introduces capabilities such as In Network Compute and Chiplet Definition for open, scale out AI infrastructure.
  • This development targets lower latency, better distributed AI workload scaling, and reduced vendor lock in for data center and cloud operators.

Advanced Micro Devices, trading at around $236.63, has seen strong share price momentum, with a 1 year return of 166.8% and a 3 year return of 157.0%. The stock is also up 8.8% over the past week and 16.4% over the past month, while the year to date return stands at 5.9%. These figures underline how closely investors are watching AMD’s role in AI infrastructure and data center technologies.

For readers, the UALink ratification highlights AMD’s involvement in open standards aimed at multi vendor AI deployments, where interoperability and flexibility are important. As AI workloads become more distributed across accelerators and data centers, developments around open interconnects like UALink may influence how platforms that include AMD hardware fit into future AI build outs.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Advanced Micro Devices by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Advanced Micro Devices.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 4 things going right for Advanced Micro Devices that every investor should see.

UALink’s ratified specifications put AMD closer to the center of how large-scale AI clusters are wired together. Features such as In Network Compute and a defined chiplet specification are aimed at making accelerator clusters more efficient and easier to scale across racks and data centers. For investors, the key point is that AMD is not just supplying CPUs and GPUs. It is also helping to shape the plumbing that lets those chips work together in multi vendor environments. That can matter for customers that want to mix hardware from AMD, Nvidia, Intel and custom silicon while keeping latency, bandwidth use and manageability under tighter control.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Advanced Micro Devices Narrative

  • The narrative highlights AMD’s push to be a full stack AI compute provider, and UALink’s open, multi vendor interconnect supports that by making EPYC CPUs and Instinct accelerators easier to plug into heterogeneous clusters used by large cloud and enterprise buyers.
  • The narrative also flags competition from Nvidia, Intel and hyperscaler custom chips. An open interconnect could reduce differentiation if rivals adopt the same standard and customers treat connectivity as a commodity rather than a reason to choose AMD systems.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on product roadmaps and large GPU deals but gives less attention to standards work like UALink. This standards work may influence how quickly and broadly AI infrastructure based on AMD hardware can be deployed across different vendors and regions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Advanced Micro Devices to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Open standards can intensify competition if Nvidia, Intel and hyperscaler custom chips all plug into the same fabric, which could pressure pricing power across accelerators and supporting platforms.
  • ⚠️ AMD already carries execution risk around product rollouts, AI partnerships and high R&D spend. Adding a complex interconnect standard increases the technical and ecosystem coordination needed to meet customer expectations.
  • 🎁 An open, multi vendor interconnect that reduces vendor lock in can appeal to large cloud and data center operators, potentially supporting demand for AMD’s CPUs and GPUs in mixed hardware fleets.
  • 🎁 UALink’s focus on distributed AI workload scaling, manageability and chiplet support fits with AMD’s emphasis on data center and AI growth, aligning its hardware roadmaps with how customers are building next generation clusters.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to watch how quickly UALink moves from specification to real deployments that include AMD hardware, and whether major cloud providers publicly back the standard with production systems. Adoption by ecosystem partners, such as networking vendors and system integrators, will also matter for seeing if UALink becomes a default choice for large AI clusters or remains one of several competing options. Any future product announcements from AMD that explicitly reference UALink based systems could give additional clues on how tightly this standard is woven into its AI and data center growth plans.
